function mi_inference(estimates, variances; α = 0.05)
    rules = rubin_rules(estimates, variances)
    
    # t-statistic and p-value for testing H₀: θ = 0
    t_stat = rules.estimate / sqrt(rules.variance)
    p_value = 2 * (1 - cdf(TDist(rules.df), abs(t_stat)))
    
    # Confidence interval
    t_crit = quantile(TDist(rules.df), 1 - α/2)
    ci_lower = rules.estimate - t_crit * sqrt(rules.variance)
    ci_upper = rules.estimate + t_crit * sqrt(rules.variance)
    
    return (
        estimate = rules.estimate,
        std_error = sqrt(rules.variance),
        t_statistic = t_stat,
        p_value = p_value,
        ci_lower = ci_lower,
        ci_upper = ci_upper,
        df = rules.df,
        fmi = rules.fmi
    )
end

# Example: Combining results from 5 imputations
estimates = [2.15, 2.08, 2.23, 2.11, 2.19]
variances = [0.16, 0.18, 0.15, 0.17, 0.16]

results = mi_inference(estimates, variances)
println("MI Estimate: $(round(results.estimate, digits=3))")
println("Standard Error: $(round(results.std_error, digits=3))")
println("95% CI: [$(round(results.ci_lower, digits=3)), $(round(results.ci_upper, digits=3))]")
println("Fraction Missing Information: $(round(results.fmi, digits=3))")

6.3 7.3 Imputation Models

The success of multiple imputation critically depends on the specification of appropriate imputation models that capture the relationships between variables and generate plausible values for missing data. Several approaches have been developed, each with distinct advantages and computational characteristics.

6.3.1 Joint Modeling Approach

The joint modeling approach specifies a single multivariate model for all variables containing missing values. For continuous variables, this often takes the form of a multivariate normal distribution.

Definition 7.3 (Joint Multivariate Normal Imputation). Let Y = (Y₁, Y₂, …, Yₚ) follow a multivariate normal distribution with mean μ and covariance Σ. The imputation model is:

Y ~ N(μ, Σ)

Missing values are drawn from their conditional distribution given observed values.

For mixed continuous and categorical variables, joint modeling becomes more complex, often requiring specialized distributions like the general location model or latent variable approaches.

6.3.2 Fully Conditional Specification (FCS/MICE)

Fully Conditional Specification, also known as Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations (MICE), specifies a separate conditional model for each variable containing missing values.

Definition 7.4 (Fully Conditional Specification). For variables Y₁, Y₂, …, Yₚ, FCS specifies conditional models:

Y₁ | Y₂, Y₃, …, Yₚ, X ~ f₁(·) Y₂ | Y₁, Y₃, …, Yₚ, X ~ f₂(·) ⋮ Yₚ | Y₁, Y₂, …, Yₚ₋₁, X ~ fₚ(·)

The imputation proceeds by Gibbs sampling, cycling through variables and drawing from each conditional distribution.

The flexibility of FCS allows for appropriate modeling of different variable types: - Continuous variables: Linear regression - Binary variables: Logistic regression
- Ordinal variables: Proportional odds models - Count variables: Poisson or negative binomial regression

using MLJ, MLJLinearModels, Random
using DataFrames, StatsBase

"""
    mice_imputation(data, n_imputations=5, n_iterations=10)

Perform Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations (MICE).
Assumes data is a DataFrame with missing values coded as `missing`.
"""
function mice_imputation(data::DataFrame; n_imputations::Int=5, n_iterations::Int=10)
    n_obs, n_vars = size(data)
    var_names = names(data)
    
    # Identify variables with missing values
    missing_vars = [var for var in var_names if any(ismissing.(data[!, var]))]
    complete_vars = setdiff(var_names, missing_vars)
    
    imputed_datasets = Vector{DataFrame}(undef, n_imputations)
    
    for imp in 1:n_imputations
        # Initialize with mean/mode imputation
        current_data = copy(data)
        for var in missing_vars
            if eltype(current_data[!, var]) <: Union{Missing, Number}
                # Continuous variable - use mean
                mean_val = mean(skipmissing(current_data[!, var]))
                current_data[!, var] = coalesce.(current_data[!, var], mean_val)
            else
                # Categorical variable - use mode
                mode_val = mode(skipmissing(current_data[!, var]))
                current_data[!, var] = coalesce.(current_data[!, var], mode_val)
            end
        end
        
        # MICE iterations
        for iter in 1:n_iterations
            for var in missing_vars
                # Find missing observations for this variable
                missing_idx = findall(ismissing.(data[!, var]))
                
                if isempty(missing_idx)
                    continue
                end
                
                # Prepare predictor variables (all other variables)
                predictor_vars = setdiff(var_names, [var])
                X = current_data[!, predictor_vars]
                y = current_data[!, var]
                
                # Fit imputation model on observed cases
                observed_idx = findall(.!ismissing.(data[!, var]))
                X_obs = X[observed_idx, :]
                y_obs = y[observed_idx]
                
                if eltype(data[!, var]) <: Union{Missing, Number}
                    # Linear regression for continuous variables
                    model = LinearRegressor()
                    mach = machine(model, X_obs, y_obs)
                    fit!(mach, verbosity=0)
                    
                    # Predict for missing observations
                    X_mis = X[missing_idx, :]
                    y_pred = predict(mach, X_mis)
                    
                    # Add random error to predictions
                    residuals = y_obs .- predict(mach, X_obs)
                    sigma_hat = std(residuals)
                    y_imputed = y_pred .+ randn(length(y_pred)) .* sigma_hat
                    
                    current_data[missing_idx, var] = y_imputed
                else
                    # Logistic regression for binary/categorical variables
                    model = LogisticClassifier()
                    mach = machine(model, X_obs, y_obs)
                    fit!(mach, verbosity=0)
                    
                    # Sample from predicted probabilities
                    X_mis = X[missing_idx, :]
                    prob_dist = predict(mach, X_mis)
                    y_imputed = [rand(d) for d in prob_dist]
                    
                    current_data[missing_idx, var] = y_imputed
                end
            end
        end
        
        imputed_datasets[imp] = current_data
    end
    
    return imputed_datasets
end

# Example usage with simulated data
Random.seed!(123)
n = 200
df = DataFrame(
    x1 = randn(n),
    x2 = randn(n),
    y = randn(n)
)

# Create missing data (MAR pattern)
df.y[df.x1 .< -0.5] .= missing
df.x2[df.x1 .> 1.0] .= missing

println("Original data missingness pattern:")
println("y missing: $(sum(ismissing.(df.y))) observations")
println("x2 missing: $(sum(ismissing.(df.x2))) observations")

# Perform MICE imputation
imputed_data = mice_imputation(df, n_imputations=5, n_iterations=10)
println("\nCreated $(length(imputed_data)) imputed datasets")

6.3.3 Predictive Mean Matching

Predictive Mean Matching (PMM) provides a semi-parametric alternative that maintains the observed data distribution while still using regression relationships for imputation.

Definition 7.5 (Predictive Mean Matching). For a variable Y with missing values: 1. Fit a regression model Y = Xβ + ε to observed data 2. For each missing value Yᵢ, find the k nearest observed values in terms of predicted values X̂ᵢβ̂ 3. Randomly select one of these k donors and use its observed Y value as the imputed value

PMM has several advantages: - Preserves the distribution of observed values - Handles non-normal distributions naturally
- Provides realistic values (actual observed values) - Robust to model misspecification

using NearestNeighbors

"""
    predictive_mean_matching(y_obs, X_obs, X_mis; k=5)

Perform predictive mean matching imputation.

Arguments:
- y_obs: observed values of variable to impute
- X_obs: predictor matrix for observed cases
- X_mis: predictor matrix for missing cases
- k: number of nearest neighbors to consider
"""
function predictive_mean_matching(y_obs::Vector{Float64}, 
                                 X_obs::Matrix{Float64}, 
                                 X_mis::Matrix{Float64}; 
                                 k::Int=5)
    n_obs = length(y_obs)
    n_mis = size(X_mis, 1)
    
    # Fit regression model to observed data
    β_hat = X_obs \ y_obs
    
    # Compute predicted values
    y_pred_obs = X_obs * β_hat
    y_pred_mis = X_mis * β_hat
    
    # Build KD-tree for nearest neighbor search
    kdtree = KDTree(reshape(y_pred_obs, 1, :))
    
    # Impute missing values
    y_imputed = Vector{Float64}(undef, n_mis)
    
    for i in 1:n_mis
        # Find k nearest neighbors based on predicted values
        indices, distances = knn(kdtree, [y_pred_mis[i]], k)
        
        # Randomly select one donor from the k nearest neighbors
        donor_idx = rand(indices[1])
        y_imputed[i] = y_obs[donor_idx]
    end
    
    return y_imputed
end

# Example: PMM imputation
Random.seed!(456)
n_complete = 150
X_complete = [ones(n_complete) randn(n_complete, 2)]
y_complete = X_complete * [1.0, 2.0, -1.5] + 0.5 * randn(n_complete)

# Create some missing values
n_missing = 50
X_missing = [ones(n_missing) randn(n_missing, 2)]

# Perform PMM
y_imputed_pmm = predictive_mean_matching(y_complete, X_complete, X_missing, k=3)

println("PMM Imputation completed")
println("Mean of imputed values: $(round(mean(y_imputed_pmm), digits=3))")
println("Standard deviation: $(round(std(y_imputed_pmm), digits=3))")

6.4 7.4 Congeniality and Compatibility

The relationship between the imputation model and the analysis model is crucial for the validity of multiple imputation inferences. Two key concepts govern this relationship: congeniality and compatibility.

Definition 7.6 (Congeniality). An imputation model is congenial with respect to an analysis model if the imputation model includes all variables that appear in the analysis model, with functional relationships that are at least as general as those assumed in the analysis model.

Definition 7.7 (Compatibility). An imputation model is compatible with an analysis model if both models could simultaneously be true for the same data-generating process.

Uncongeniality occurs when the imputation model is less general than the analysis model, potentially leading to biased results. Common sources of uncongeniality include:

  • Missing variables: The imputation model omits variables that appear in the analysis model
  • Functional form: The imputation model assumes linear relationships when the analysis model includes interactions or non-linear terms
  • Distributional assumptions: The imputation model assumes normality when the analysis model allows for other distributions

6.4.1 Strategies for Ensuring Congeniality

Several strategies help ensure congeniality between imputation and analysis models:

  1. Include all analysis variables: The imputation model should include all variables that appear in the analysis model, even if some are completely observed.

  2. Match functional forms: If the analysis model includes interactions or transformations, these should be included in the imputation model or the transformed variables should be imputed directly.

  3. Use flexible imputation models: Non-parametric or semi-parametric imputation methods (like PMM) can provide robustness against model misspecification.

  4. Impute then transform vs. transform then impute: The choice depends on the nature of the transformation and the missing data pattern.

Theorem 7.2 (Congeniality and Bias). Under MAR and proper imputation, if the imputation model is congenial with the analysis model, then multiple imputation provides consistent estimates of analysis model parameters as both sample size and number of imputations approach infinity.

Proof. Congeniality ensures that the imputation model preserves the relationships assumed in the analysis model. Under MAR, the missing data mechanism does not depend on unobserved values. Proper imputation incorporates parameter uncertainty. These conditions together ensure that the MI procedure targets the correct inferential distribution. □

"""
    assess_congeniality(analysis_vars, imputation_data, analysis_model)

Assess congeniality between imputation and analysis models by comparing
variable relationships across imputed datasets.
"""
function assess_congeniality_check(analysis_vars::Vector{String}, 
                                  imputed_datasets::Vector{DataFrame})
    n_imputations = length(imputed_datasets)
    
    println("Congeniality Assessment:")
    println("=" ^ 50)
    
    # Check variable inclusion
    all_vars_present = all(var in names(imputed_datasets[1]) for var in analysis_vars)
    println("All analysis variables present: $all_vars_present")
    
    if !all_vars_present
        missing_vars = setdiff(analysis_vars, names(imputed_datasets[1]))
        println("Missing variables: $missing_vars")
    end
    
    # Compare correlations across imputations
    correlations = []
    for dataset in imputed_datasets
        numeric_vars = analysis_vars[analysis_vars .∈ Ref(names(dataset))]
        numeric_data = dataset[!, numeric_vars]
        
        # Remove non-numeric columns for correlation computation
        numeric_cols = [col for col in names(numeric_data) 
                       if eltype(numeric_data[!, col]) <: Number]
        
        if length(numeric_cols) >= 2
            cor_matrix = cor(Matrix(numeric_data[!, numeric_cols]))
            push!(correlations, cor_matrix)
        end
    end
    
    if !isempty(correlations) && length(correlations[1]) > 1
        # Compute between-imputation variability in correlations
        avg_cor = mean(correlations)
        var_cor = var(correlations)
        
        println("\nCorrelation stability across imputations:")
        println("Mean absolute correlation variability: $(round(mean(abs.(var_cor)), digits=4))")
        
        # Flag potentially problematic correlations
        high_var_threshold = 0.1
        problem_pairs = findall(abs.(var_cor) .> high_var_threshold)
        if !isempty(problem_pairs)
            println("High variability correlations detected at indices: $problem_pairs")
        end
    end
    
    return all_vars_present
end

6.5 7.5 Number of Imputations

The choice of the number of imputations M represents a balance between computational efficiency and statistical accuracy. Early recommendations suggested M = 5 was sufficient, but modern understanding and computational capabilities support larger values.

6.5.1 Traditional Rule (M = 5)

Rubin’s original recommendation of M = 5 was based on efficiency considerations under the assumption of moderate missing information rates (λ < 0.5). The relative efficiency of M imputations compared to infinite imputations is:

RE(M) = (1 + λ/M)⁻¹

For λ = 0.3 and M = 5, the relative efficiency is approximately 94%, which seemed adequate for most applications.

6.5.2 Modern Recommendations

Contemporary guidance suggests larger values of M for several reasons:

  1. Reproducibility: Larger M reduces Monte Carlo variation in results
  2. High missing information: When λ > 0.5, M = 5 provides poor efficiency
  3. Complex estimands: Non-linear functions of parameters may require larger M
  4. Computational feasibility: Modern computing makes larger M practical

Theorem 7.3 (Optimal Number of Imputations). For a given computational budget, the optimal number of imputations M* minimizes the total variance of the MI estimator, which includes both statistical variance and Monte Carlo variance from finite M.

Proof. The total mean squared error of the MI estimator can be decomposed as MSE = (statistical variance) + (Monte Carlo variance). The statistical variance decreases with sample size but is independent of M for M sufficiently large. The Monte Carlo variance is approximately λ²/(M-1). For fixed computational cost C = M × (cost per imputation), minimizing total MSE yields M* proportional to λ/√(cost per imputation). □

Current recommendations suggest: - M ≥ 20 for routine use - M ≥ 100 when λ > 0.7
- M = 100-200 for final analyses in important studies

"""
    imputation_efficiency(λ, M_values)

Compute the relative efficiency of different numbers of imputations
for given fraction of missing information λ.
"""
function imputation_efficiency(λ::Float64, M_values::Vector{Int})
    efficiency = [(1 + λ/M)^(-1) for M in M_values]
    
    println("Relative Efficiency by Number of Imputations")
    println("λ (Fraction Missing Information) = $λ")
    println("-" ^ 40)
    for (M, eff) in zip(M_values, efficiency)
        println("M = $(lpad(M, 3)): $(round(eff*100, digits=1))%")
    end
    
    return efficiency
end

"""
    monte_carlo_error(estimates_matrix)

Assess Monte Carlo error across different numbers of imputations.
estimates_matrix: each column contains estimates from one "study",
each row corresponds to a different M.
"""
function monte_carlo_error(estimates_matrix::Matrix{Float64})
    n_studies = size(estimates_matrix, 2)
    M_values = collect(1:size(estimates_matrix, 1))
    
    # Compute standard deviation across studies for each M
    mc_errors = [std(estimates_matrix[i, :]) for i in 1:length(M_values)]
    
    println("Monte Carlo Standard Error by Number of Imputations")
    println("-" ^ 50)
    for (M, error) in zip(M_values, mc_errors)
        println("M = $(lpad(M, 3)): $(round(error, digits=4))")
    end
    
    return mc_errors
end

# Example: Compare efficiency for different missing information rates
λ_values = [0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9]
M_range = [5, 10, 20, 50, 100]

println("Efficiency Analysis:")
for λ in λ_values
    println("\nλ = $λ")
    imputation_efficiency(λ, M_range)
end

6.6 7.6 Diagnostics for Imputation Models

Proper diagnostic assessment ensures that imputation models adequately capture the relationships in the data and generate plausible values for missing observations. Multiple classes of diagnostics address different aspects of imputation quality.

6.6.1 Distributional Diagnostics

The imputed values should follow distributions consistent with the observed data, accounting for any covariates that influence the outcome distribution.

using Plots, StatsPlots

"""
    plot_imputation_diagnostics(original_data, imputed_datasets, variable)

Create diagnostic plots comparing observed and imputed value distributions.
"""
function plot_imputation_diagnostics(original_data::DataFrame, 
                                    imputed_datasets::Vector{DataFrame}, 
                                    variable::String)
    
    # Extract observed values
    observed_vals = skipmissing(original_data[!, variable]) |> collect
    
    # Extract imputed values from each dataset
    imputed_vals = []
    for dataset in imputed_datasets
        # Find originally missing positions
        missing_mask = ismissing.(original_data[!, variable])
        push!(imputed_vals, dataset[missing_mask, variable])
    end
    all_imputed = vcat(imputed_vals...)
    
    # Create comparison plots
    p1 = density(observed_vals, label="Observed", linewidth=2, 
                title="Distribution Comparison: $variable")
    density!(p1, all_imputed, label="Imputed", linewidth=2, alpha=0.7)
    
    # Q-Q plot for distributional comparison
    n_compare = min(length(observed_vals), length(all_imputed))
    obs_quantiles = quantile(observed_vals, range(0.01, 0.99, length=n_compare))
    imp_quantiles = quantile(all_imputed, range(0.01, 0.99, length=n_compare))
    
    p2 = scatter(obs_quantiles, imp_quantiles, alpha=0.6, 
                title="Q-Q Plot", xlabel="Observed Quantiles", 
                ylabel="Imputed Quantiles", legend=false)
    plot!(p2, [minimum(obs_quantiles), maximum(obs_quantiles)], 
          [minimum(obs_quantiles), maximum(obs_quantiles)], 
          color=:red, linestyle=:dash)
    
    return plot(p1, p2, layout=(1,2), size=(800, 300))
end

"""
    convergence_diagnostics(imputed_datasets, variable)

Assess MICE convergence by examining the trace plots of imputed values.
"""
function convergence_diagnostics(mice_chains::Vector{Vector{Float64}}, 
                               variable_name::String)
    n_iterations = length(mice_chains)
    n_imputed = length(mice_chains[1])
    
    # Create trace plots for a subset of imputed values
    n_traces = min(10, n_imputed)
    selected_indices = rand(1:n_imputed, n_traces)
    
    trace_data = Matrix{Float64}(undef, n_iterations, n_traces)
    for (i, chain) in enumerate(mice_chains)
        trace_data[i, :] = chain[selected_indices]
    end
    
    p = plot(title="MICE Convergence: $variable_name", 
            xlabel="Iteration", ylabel="Imputed Value")
    
    for j in 1:n_traces
        plot!(p, 1:n_iterations, trace_data[:, j], alpha=0.6, legend=false)
    end
    
    return p
end

6.6.2 Pattern Diagnostics

Pattern-mixture diagnostics examine whether the imputation model adequately captures differences between cases with different missing data patterns.

Definition 7.8 (Pattern-Mixture Diagnostic). For variables with missing data, compare the distribution of imputed values across different missing data patterns. Substantial differences may indicate inadequate modeling of pattern-specific effects.

6.6.3 Model Fit Diagnostics

Standard regression diagnostics can be applied to each conditional model in MICE to assess fit quality.

"""
    mice_model_diagnostics(data, variable, predictors)

Perform regression diagnostics for a single variable's imputation model in MICE.
"""
function mice_model_diagnostics(data::DataFrame, variable::String, 
                               predictors::Vector{String})
    # Extract complete cases for the regression
    complete_mask = .!ismissing.(data[!, variable])
    y = data[complete_mask, variable]
    X = data[complete_mask, predictors]
    
    # Convert to matrix form
    X_matrix = Matrix{Float64}(X)
    X_with_intercept = [ones(length(y)) X_matrix]
    
    # Fit linear regression
    β = X_with_intercept \ y
    y_pred = X_with_intercept * β
    residuals = y - y_pred
    
    # Diagnostic plots
    p1 = scatter(y_pred, residuals, alpha=0.6, 
                title="Residuals vs Fitted", 
                xlabel="Fitted Values", ylabel="Residuals")
    hline!(p1, [0], color=:red, linestyle=:dash)
    
    p2 = qqplot(Normal(0, 1), residuals./std(residuals), 
               title="Normal Q-Q Plot of Residuals")
    
    # R-squared
    ss_res = sum(residuals.^2)
    ss_tot = sum((y .- mean(y)).^2)
    r_squared = 1 - ss_res / ss_tot
    
    println("Model diagnostics for $variable:")
    println("R-squared: $(round


\newpage

# Chapter 8: Joint Models for Longitudinal and Time-to-Event Data

Longitudinal studies frequently collect both repeated biomarker measurements and information about the occurrence and timing of clinical events. Traditional approaches analyze these outcomes separately, ignoring their inherent association. Joint modeling provides a principled framework for simultaneously analyzing longitudinal and time-to-event data, accounting for their correlation through shared random effects or other coupling mechanisms.

## 8.1 Motivation and Model Framework

Consider a clinical study where patients provide repeated measurements of a biomarker (e.g., prostate-specific antigen levels, CD4+ cell counts) and are followed until the occurrence of an event (death, disease progression, dropout). The longitudinal trajectory and event process are intrinsically linked: patients with declining biomarker values may have higher hazard rates, while patients who experience events early may exhibit different longitudinal patterns before event occurrence.

The fundamental insight of joint modeling is to specify a shared parameter structure that captures this association. The most common approach uses shared random effects to link a linear mixed model for the longitudinal process with a survival model for the time-to-event outcome.

> **Definition 8.1 (Shared Random Effects Joint Model).** A shared random effects joint model consists of:
> 
> 1. **Longitudinal submodel**: A linear mixed model
>    $$y_i(t) = m_i(t) + \epsilon_i(t) = \mathbf{x}_i^T(t)\boldsymbol{\beta} + \mathbf{z}_i^T(t)\mathbf{b}_i + \epsilon_i(t)$$
>    where $\mathbf{b}_i \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{D})$ and $\epsilon_i(t) \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.
> 
> 2. **Survival submodel**: A proportional hazards model
>    $$h_i(t \mid \mathcal{M}_i(t), \mathbf{w}_i) = h_0(t) \exp\{\boldsymbol{\gamma}^T \mathbf{w}_i + \alpha m_i(t)\}$$
>    where $\mathcal{M}_i(t) = \{m_i(s), 0 \leq s < t\}$ represents the longitudinal history and $\alpha$ quantifies the association.

The parameter $\alpha$ measures the log-hazard ratio per unit increase in the current true value of the longitudinal outcome. When $\alpha > 0$, higher biomarker values increase the hazard; when $\alpha < 0$, higher values are protective.

### 8.1.1 Association Structures

While the current value parameterization $\alpha m_i(t)$ is most common, several alternatives exist:

- **Slope association**: $\alpha m_i'(t)$ where $m_i'(t) = \frac{d}{dt}m_i(t)$
- **Area under the curve**: $\alpha \int_0^t m_i(s) ds$
- **Lagged association**: $\alpha m_i(t-\tau)$ for some lag $\tau > 0$
- **Multiple associations**: $\alpha_1 m_i(t) + \alpha_2 m_i'(t)$

## 8.2 Likelihood Construction and Identifiability

The joint likelihood for subject $i$ involves integrating over the random effects distribution. Let $T_i$ denote the observed event time, $\delta_i$ the event indicator, and $\mathbf{y}_i = \{y_i(t_{ij}), j = 1, \ldots, n_i\}$ the longitudinal observations.

> **Theorem 8.1 (Joint Likelihood).** Under the conditional independence assumption that longitudinal and survival outcomes are independent given the random effects, the joint likelihood contribution for subject $i$ is:
> $$L_i(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \int p(\mathbf{y}_i \mid \mathbf{b}_i; \boldsymbol{\theta}_y) \cdot p(T_i, \delta_i \mid \mathbf{b}_i; \boldsymbol{\theta}_s) \cdot p(\mathbf{b}_i; \boldsymbol{\theta}_b) \, d\mathbf{b}_i$$
> where $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\boldsymbol{\theta}_y, \boldsymbol{\theta}_s, \boldsymbol{\theta}_b)$ encompasses all model parameters.

*Proof.* The conditional independence assumption gives:
$$p(\mathbf{y}_i, T_i, \delta_i \mid \mathbf{b}_i) = p(\mathbf{y}_i \mid \mathbf{b}_i) p(T_i, \delta_i \mid \mathbf{b}_i)$$

Integrating over the random effects distribution yields the marginal likelihood. The survival component involves the hazard function and survival function:
$$p(T_i, \delta_i \mid \mathbf{b}_i) = h_i(T_i \mid \mathbf{b}_i)^{\delta_i} S_i(T_i \mid \mathbf{b}_i)$$
where $S_i(t \mid \mathbf{b}_i) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t h_i(s \mid \mathbf{b}_i) ds\right)$.  □

### 8.2.1 Identifiability Considerations

Joint models face identifiability challenges, particularly when the association parameter $\alpha$ is large or when there is substantial measurement error in the longitudinal process. 

> **Theorem 8.2 (Identifiability Conditions).** The joint model parameters are identifiable if:
> 1. The longitudinal submodel is identifiable
> 2. The baseline hazard $h_0(t)$ is identifiable (typically through a parametric assumption or sufficient events)
> 3. There exists variation in the longitudinal trajectories among subjects who experience events

*Proof sketch.* Conditions 1 and 2 ensure standard identifiability for the separate submodels. Condition 3 prevents the association parameter $\alpha$ from being confounded with the baseline hazard. Without variation in longitudinal values at event times, any value of $\alpha$ could be offset by adjusting $h_0(t)$.  □

## 8.3 Estimation via EM Algorithm

The integral in the joint likelihood typically lacks a closed form, necessitating numerical integration or Monte Carlo methods. The EM algorithm provides a natural framework, treating the random effects as missing data.

### 8.3.1 Gauss-Hermite Quadrature

For moderate-dimensional random effects, Gauss-Hermite quadrature offers accurate numerical integration. Let $\mathbf{b}_i = \sqrt{2}\mathbf{D}^{1/2}\mathbf{z} + \boldsymbol{\mu}_b$ where $\mathbf{z} \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$.

> **Algorithm 8.1 (EM with Gauss-Hermite Quadrature).**
> 
> **E-step**: Compute posterior distributions
> $$w_{ik} = \frac{\pi_k p(\mathbf{y}_i, T_i, \delta_i \mid \mathbf{z}_k)}{\sum_{l=1}^K \pi_l p(\mathbf{y}_i, T_i, \delta_i \mid \mathbf{z}_l)}$$
> where $\mathbf{z}_k$ and $\pi_k$ are quadrature points and weights.
> 
> **M-step**: Maximize the weighted complete-data likelihood
> $$Q(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}^{(m)}) = \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{k=1}^K w_{ik}^{(m)} \log p(\mathbf{y}_i, T_i, \delta_i, \mathbf{z}_k \mid \boldsymbol{\theta})$$

```julia
using Distributions, LinearAlgebra, Optimization, OptimizationOptimJL

function gauss_hermite_nodes(n::Int)
    # Generate Gauss-Hermite quadrature nodes and weights
    # This is a simplified version - use GaussQuadrature.jl in practice
    if n == 1
        return [0.0], [sqrt(π)]
    elseif n == 2
        nodes = [-sqrt(2)/2, sqrt(2)/2]
        weights = [sqrt(π)/2, sqrt(π)/2]
        return nodes, weights
    else
        # Use standard algorithm for higher order
        error("Use GaussQuadrature.jl for n > 2")
    end
end

function joint_model_em_step(y_data, t_data, δ_data, X_long, Z_long, W_surv, 
                             β, γ, α, σ², D, h₀; max_iter=100, tol=1e-6)
    n = length(y_data)
    q = size(Z_long[1], 2)  # Random effects dimension
    
    # Gauss-Hermite quadrature setup
    nodes_1d, weights_1d = gauss_hermite_nodes(5)
    
    # Create multidimensional quadrature (for q-dimensional random effects)
    # This is simplified for q=1 case
    if q == 1
        nodes = nodes_1d
        weights = weights_1d
    else
        error("Multidimensional quadrature not implemented in this example")
    end
    
    K = length(nodes)
    θ_old = (β=β, γ=γ, α=α, σ²=σ², D=D)
    
    for iter in 1:max_iter
        # E-step: Compute posterior weights
        w = zeros(n, K)
        
        for i in 1:n
            for k in 1:K
                b_k = sqrt(2 * D[1,1]) * nodes[k]  # Transform to original scale
                
                # Longitudinal likelihood contribution
                μ_long = X_long[i] * β + Z_long[i] * [b_k]
                ll_long = sum(logpdf.(Normal.(μ_long, sqrt(σ²)), y_data[i]))
                
                # Survival likelihood contribution  
                η_surv = W_surv[i] ⋅ γ + α * (X_long[i][end, :] ⋅ β + Z_long[i][end, :] * [b_k])
                hazard = h₀ * exp(η_surv)
                
                if δ_data[i] == 1  # Event observed
                    ll_surv = log(hazard) - hazard * t_data[i]
                else  # Censored
                    ll_surv = -hazard * t_data[i]
                end
                
                # Prior for random effects (standard normal after transformation)
                ll_prior = logpdf(Normal(0, 1), nodes[k])
                
                w[i, k] = exp(ll_long + ll_surv + ll_prior) * weights[k]
            end
            
            # Normalize weights
            w[i, :] ./= sum(w[i, :])
        end
        
        # M-step: Update parameters (simplified)
        # In practice, this would involve solving weighted normal equations
        # and optimizing the baseline hazard
        
        # Check convergence
        # θ_new = ... (update parameters)
        # if norm([θ_new...] - [θ_old...]) < tol
        #     break
        # end
        # θ_old = θ_new
    end
    
    return θ_old, w
end

6.6.4 8.3.2 MCMC Estimation

For high-dimensional random effects or complex association structures, Markov Chain Monte Carlo provides greater flexibility. Bayesian estimation naturally accommodates uncertainty quantification.

using Turing, StatsModels

@model function joint_model_mcmc(y_obs, t_obs, δ_obs, X_long, Z_long, W_surv)
    n = length(y_obs)
    p = size(X_long[1], 2)
    q = size(Z_long[1], 2)
    r = size(W_surv[1], 1)
    
    # Priors
    β ~ MvNormal(zeros(p), 10.0 * I(p))
    γ ~ MvNormal(zeros(r), 10.0 * I(r))
    α ~ Normal(0, 5)
    σ² ~ InverseGamma(2, 1)
    
    # Random effects covariance matrix (simplified for diagonal case)
    τ ~ InverseGamma(2, 1)
    D = diagm(fill(τ, q))
    
    # Baseline hazard (Weibull)
    λ ~ Gamma(2, 1)  # Scale parameter
    κ ~ Gamma(2, 1)  # Shape parameter
    
    # Random effects
    b = [MvNormal(zeros(q), D) for _ in 1:n]
    
    # Longitudinal submodel
    for i in 1:n
        μ_i = X_long[i] * β + Z_long[i] * b[i]
        y_obs[i] ~ MvNormal(μ_i, σ² * I(length(μ_i)))
    end
    
    # Survival submodel
    for i in 1:n
        # Current value at event/censoring time
        m_i_T = X_long[i][end, :] ⋅ β + Z_long[i][end, :] ⋅ b[i]
        
        # Linear predictor
        η_i = W_surv[i] ⋅ γ + α * m_i_T
        
        # Weibull hazard: h(t) = κλ(λt)^(κ-1) * exp(η)
        # Survival function: S(t) = exp(-(λt)^κ * exp(η))
        
        if δ_obs[i] == 1  # Event observed
            # Density: h(t) * S(t)
            log_hazard = log(κ) + log(λ) + (κ-1)*log(λ*t_obs[i]) + η_i
            log_survival = -(λ*t_obs[i])^κ * exp(η_i)
            Turing.@addlogprob!(log_hazard + log_survival)
        else  # Censored
            # Survival function only
            log_survival = -(λ*t_obs[i])^κ * exp(η_i)
            Turing.@addlogprob!(log_survival)
        end
    end
end

# Fitting function
function fit_joint_model_mcmc(y_data, t_data, δ_data, X_long, Z_long, W_surv;
                             n_samples=2000, n_chains=4)
    model = joint_model_mcmc(y_data, t_data, δ_data, X_long, Z_long, W_surv)
    chain = sample(model, NUTS(), MCMCThreads(), n_samples, n_chains)
    return chain
end

6.7 8.4 Dynamic Predictions

A key advantage of joint models is their ability to provide dynamic predictions: as new longitudinal measurements become available for a subject, we can update their predicted survival probability in real-time.

Definition 8.2 (Dynamic Prediction). For a subject with longitudinal history \(\mathcal{H}_i(s) = \{y_i(t_{ij}), t_{ij} \leq s\}\) observed up to time \(s\), the conditional survival probability at time \(u > s\) is: \[\pi_i(u \mid s) = P(T_i > u \mid T_i > s, \mathcal{H}_i(s))\]

6.7.1 8.4.1 Monte Carlo Integration

Dynamic predictions require integration over the posterior distribution of random effects given the observed longitudinal history:

\[\pi_i(u \mid s) = \int S_i(u \mid \mathbf{b}_i) p(\mathbf{b}_i \mid \mathcal{H}_i(s), T_i > s) d\mathbf{b}_i\]

function dynamic_prediction(joint_model_fit, new_y, new_times, prediction_time, 
                           current_time; n_samples=1000)
    """
    Compute dynamic survival prediction using Monte Carlo integration
    
    Args:
        joint_model_fit: Fitted joint model (MCMC chain or EM result)
        new_y: Vector of new longitudinal observations
        new_times: Vector of observation times
        prediction_time: Time point for survival prediction
        current_time: Current time (last observation time)
        n_samples: Number of Monte Carlo samples
    """
    
    # Extract parameter samples from fitted model
    if isa(joint_model_fit, Chains)  # MCMC chain
        β_samples = Array(joint_model_fit[:β])
        α_samples = vec(joint_model_fit[:α])
        σ²_samples = vec(joint_model_fit[:σ²])
        τ_samples = vec(joint_model_fit[:τ])
        # ... extract other parameters
    else  # EM estimates (point estimates)
        error("EM dynamic predictions require sampling from asymptotic distribution")
    end
    
    n_posterior_samples = length(α_samples)
    survival_probs = Float64[]
    
    for iter in 1:n_posterior_samples
        # Current parameter values
        β = β_samples[iter, :]
        α = α_samples[iter]
        σ² = σ²_samples[iter]
        D = diagm(fill(τ_samples[iter], length(β)))  # Simplified
        
        # Sample random effects given observed longitudinal data
        # This requires MCMC or importance sampling
        b_samples = sample_random_effects_posterior(new_y, new_times, β, σ², D; 
                                                   n_samples=50)
        
        # For each random effect sample, compute survival probability
        surv_probs_b = Float64[]
        
        for b in b_samples
            # Compute current longitudinal value
            # Assumes simple linear trajectory: m(t) = β₀ + β₁*t + b₀ + b₁*t
            m_current = β[1] + β[2]*current_time + b[1] + b[2]*current_time
            m_future = β[1] + β[2]*prediction_time + b[1] + b[2]*prediction_time
            
            # Cumulative hazard from current_time to prediction_time
            # For Weibull baseline: Λ₀(t) = (λt)^κ
            # Total hazard: Λ(t) = Λ₀(t) * exp(α * m(t))
            
            # Simplified calculation assuming constant hazard over interval
            avg_m = (m_current + m_future) / 2
            λ = 0.1  # Would use posterior samples in practice
            κ = 1.5  # Would use posterior samples in practice
            
            cum_hazard = ((λ * prediction_time)^κ - (λ * current_time)^κ) * exp(α * avg_m)
            surv_prob = exp(-cum_hazard)
            push!(surv_probs_b, surv_prob)
        end
        
        # Average over random effects samples
        push!(survival_probs, mean(surv_probs_b))
    end
    
    # Return posterior summary
    return (
        mean = mean(survival_probs),
        median = median(survival_probs),
        lower_ci = quantile(survival_probs, 0.025),
        upper_ci = quantile(survival_probs, 0.975),
        samples = survival_probs
    )
end

function sample_random_effects_posterior(y_obs, t_obs, β, σ², D; n_samples=100)
    """
    Sample from posterior distribution of random effects given longitudinal data
    Uses Metropolis-Hastings algorithm
    """
    q = size(D, 1)
    b_samples = []
    b_current = zeros(q)  # Initialize
    
    # Design matrices (simplified for linear time trend)
    X = hcat(ones(length(t_obs)), t_obs)
    Z = X  # Same as fixed effects in this example
    
    for iter in 1:n_samples
        # Propose new random effects
        b_proposal = b_current + 0.1 * randn(q)  # Tuned proposal scale
        
        # Log-likelihood for current and proposed values
        μ_current = X * β + Z * b_current
        μ_proposal = X * β + Z * b_proposal
        
        ll_current = sum(logpdf.(Normal.(μ_current, sqrt(σ²)), y_obs))
        ll_proposal = sum(logpdf.(Normal.(μ_proposal, sqrt(σ²)), y_obs))
        
        # Prior contributions
        prior_current = logpdf(MvNormal(zeros(q), D), b_current)
        prior_proposal = logpdf(MvNormal(zeros(q), D), b_proposal)
        
        # Acceptance probability
        log_α = (ll_proposal + prior_proposal) - (ll_current + prior_current)
        
        if log(rand()) < log_α
            b_current = b_proposal
        end
        
        if iter > n_samples ÷ 2  # Burn-in
            push!(b_samples, copy(b_current))
        end
    end
    
    return b_samples
end

6.7.2 8.4.2 Landmark Analysis

Dynamic predictions can be evaluated using landmark analysis, where predictions made at landmark time \(s\) are assessed among subjects who survive beyond \(s\).

function landmark_analysis(survival_times, event_indicators, longitudinal_data,
                          landmark_times, prediction_horizon; n_folds=5)
    """
    Evaluate dynamic prediction performance using landmark analysis
    """
    results = []
    
    for s in landmark_times
        # Identify subjects at risk at landmark time s
        at_risk = survival_times .>= s
        
        if sum(at_risk) < 50  # Minimum sample size
            continue
        end
        
        # Extract longitudinal data up to landmark time
        landmark_data = extract_longitudinal_history(longitudinal_data, s)
        
        # Cross-validation for unbiased evaluation
        cv_auc = Float64[]
        cv_brier = Float64[]
        
        for fold in 1:n_folds
            # Split data
            train_idx, test_idx = cv_split(sum(at_risk), fold, n_folds)
            
            # Fit model on training data
            model_fit = fit_joint_model(landmark_data[train_idx], ...)
            
            # Predict on test data
            predictions = []
            for i in test_idx
                pred = dynamic_prediction(model_fit, landmark_data[i], 
                                        s + prediction_horizon, s)
                push!(predictions, 1 - pred.mean)  # Convert to risk
            end
            
            # Evaluate predictions
            test_events = (survival_times[test_idx] .<= s + prediction_horizon) .&& 
                         (event_indicators[test_idx] .== 1)
            test_times = min.(survival_times[test_idx], s + prediction_horizon)
            
            auc = time_dependent_auc(predictions, test_events, test_times, 
                                   s + prediction_horizon)
            brier = brier_score(predictions, test_events, test_times, 
                              s + prediction_horizon)
            
            push!(cv_auc, auc)
            push!(cv_brier, brier)
        end
        
        push!(results, (
            landmark_time = s,
            prediction_time = s + prediction_horizon,
            auc_mean = mean(cv_auc),
            auc_se = std(cv_auc) / sqrt(n_folds),
            brier_mean = mean(cv_brier),
            brier_se = std(cv_brier) / sqrt(n_folds)
        ))
    end
    
    return results
end

6.8 8.5 Discrimination and Calibration

Dynamic predictions require specialized evaluation metrics that account for their time-dependent nature and the conditioning on survival to the prediction time.

6.8.1 8.5.1 Time-Dependent AUC

The area under the ROC curve must be adapted for time-to-event outcomes and dynamic predictions.

Definition 8.3 (Time-Dependent AUC). For predictions made at landmark time \(s\) for events occurring by time \(u > s\), the time-dependent AUC is: \[\text{AUC}(u|s) = P(\pi_j(u|s) > \pi_k(u|s) \mid T_j \leq u, T_k > u, T_j > s, T_k > s)\] where subject \(j\) experiences the event by time \(u\) and subject \(k\) does not.

function time_dependent_auc(risk_predictions, event_times, event_indicators, 
                           prediction_time, landmark_time)
    """
    Compute time-dependent AUC for dynamic predictions
    
    Uses inverse probability of censoring weighting (IPCW) to handle censoring
    """
    n = length(risk_predictions)
    
    # Identify comparable pairs
    # Case: event by prediction_time, survived past landmark
    # Control: survived past prediction_time, survived past landmark
    
    cases = (event_times .<= prediction_time) .&& (event_indicators .== 1) .&& 
            (event_times .> landmark_time)
    controls = (event_times .> prediction_time)
    
    if sum(cases) == 0 || sum(controls) == 0
        return NaN
    end
    
    # Simple version without IPCW weighting
    concordant = 0
    total_pairs = 0
    
    for i in findall(cases)
        for j in findall(controls)
            total_pairs += 1
            if risk_predictions[i] > risk_predictions[j]
                concordant += 1
            elseif risk_predictions[i] == risk_predictions[j]
                concordant += 0.5  # Tie handling
            end
        end
    end
    
    return total_pairs > 0 ? concordant / total_pairs : NaN
end

function brier_score(risk_predictions, event_times, event_indicators, 
                    prediction_time, landmark_time)
    """
    Compute Brier score for dynamic predictions with IPCW
    """
    n = length(risk_predictions)
    
    # Outcome at prediction time (among those who survived to landmark)
    eligible = event_times .> landmark_time
    
    if sum(eligible) == 0
        return NaN
    end
    
    outcomes = zeros(n)
    outcomes[eligible .&& (event_times .<= prediction_time) .&& (event_indicators .== 1)] .= 1
    
    # Simple Brier score (without IPCW correction for censoring)
    squared_errors = (risk_predictions[eligible] .- outcomes[eligible]).^2
    
    return mean(squared_errors)
end

6.8.2 8.5.2 Calibration Assessment

Calibration measures whether predicted probabilities match observed event rates. For dynamic predictions, this requires careful handling of the conditioning.

function calibration_plot(risk_predictions, event_times, event_indicators,
                         prediction_time, landmark_time; n_bins=10)
    """
    Create calibration plot for dynamic predictions
    """
    eligible = event_times .> landmark_time
    
    if sum(eligible) < 20
        return nothing
    end
    
    # Extract eligible data
    risks = risk_predictions[eligible]
    times = event_times[eligible]
    events = event_indicators[eligible]
    
    # Create risk bins
    risk_cuts = quantile(risks, (0:n_bins)/n_bins)
    risk_cuts[1] = 0.0  # Ensure minimum is 0
    risk_cuts[end] = 1.0  # Ensure maximum is 1
    
    bin_centers = Float64[]
    observed_rates = Float64[]
    predicted_rates = Float64[]
    bin_sizes = Int[]
    
    for i in 1:(n_bins-1)
        in_bin = (risks .>= risk_cuts[i]) .&& (risks .< risk_cuts[i+1])
        
        if i == n_bins - 1  # Include upper bound in last bin
            in_bin = in_bin .|| (risks .== risk_cuts[end])
        end
        
        if sum(in_bin) == 0
            continue
        end
        
        # Observed rate (Kaplan-Meier estimate at prediction time)
        bin_times = times[in_bin]
        bin_events = events[in_bin]
        
        # Simple observed rate calculation
        observed = sum((bin_times .<= prediction_time) .&& (bin_events .== 1))
        total = sum(in_bin)
        obs_rate = observed / total
        
        # Predicted rate (average of predictions in bin)
        pred_rate = mean(risks[in_bin])
        
        push!(bin_centers, (risk_cuts[i] + risk_cuts[i+1]) / 2)
        push!(observed_rates, obs_rate)
        push!(predicted_rates, pred_rate)
        push!(bin_sizes, total)
    end
    
    return (
        predicted = predicted_rates,
        observed = observed_rates,
        bin_centers = bin_centers,
        bin_sizes = bin_sizes
    )
end

6.9 8.6 Multiple Longitudinal Outcomes

Many clinical studies collect multiple biomarkers. Joint models can accommodate multiple longitudinal processes sharing random effects with the survival outcome.

Definition 8.4 (Multi-Outcome Joint Model). For \(K\) longitudinal outcomes, the model extends to: \[y_{ik}(t) = \mathbf{x}_{ik}^T(t)\boldsymbol{\beta}_k + \mathbf{z}_{ik}^T(t)\mathbf{b}_i + \epsilon_{ik}(t)\] \[h_i(t) = h_0(t) \exp\left\{\boldsymbol{\gamma}^T\mathbf{w}_i + \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k m_{ik}(t)\right\}\] where \(\mathbf{b}_i \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{D})\) captures correlations between outcomes and their association with survival.

```julia @model function multi_outcome_joint_model(y_obs_list, t_obs, δ_obs, X_long_list, Z_long_list, W_surv) n = length(t_obs) K = length(y_obs_list) # Number of longitudinal outcomes

# Dimensions
p = [size(X_long_list[k][1], 2) for k in 1:K]  # Fixed effects per outcome
q = size(Z_long_list[1][1], 2)  # Shared random effects dimension
r = size(W_surv[1], 1)  # Survival covariates

# Priors for longitudinal submodels
β = [MvNormal(zeros(p[k]), 10.0 * I(p[k])) for k in 1:K]
σ² = [InverseGamma(2, 1) for k in 1:K]

# Survival submodel priors
γ ~ MvNormal(zeros(r), 10.0 * I(r))
α ~ M
